policy_statement: By 2030, reduce greenhouse gas emissions from institutional, commercial, and government purchasing by at least 50%.
mechanism_description: Lowering the emissions intensity of institutional, commercial, and government purchasing reduces upstream supply-chain emissions associated with procured goods and services.
typology_evidence_quote: reduce greenhouse gas emissions from institutional, commercial, and government purchasing by at least 50%
primary_causal_pathway: Procurement choices → ↓ lifecycle/supply-chain GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) The policy changes organizational purchasing behavior by requiring or incentivizing buyers to select lower-carbon materials, products, services, vendors, or contracts. (2) This shifts market demand away from emissions-intensive production and logistics and toward suppliers with lower embodied emissions, better production processes, cleaner energy use, lower-carbon materials, or reduced transport and waste footprints. (3) The climate-relevant outcome is lower upstream lifecycle greenhouse gas emissions associated with procured goods and services, contributing to mitigation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect of policies using this mechanism is to reduce embodied supply-chain greenhouse gas emissions by shifting demand toward lower-emissions goods and services.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1. Dominant causal pathway: the core mechanism is demand-side procurement reform that lowers the emissions intensity of purchased goods and services, thereby reducing upstream lifecycle GHG emissions.  
2. Why this primary over alternatives: although some procurement reforms can also improve resource efficiency or support circularity, those are subordinate when the mechanism is explicitly defined by supply-chain emissions reduction. It is not Adaptation because it does not primarily reduce climate hazard exposure or vulnerability, and it is not Nature-Based Solutions because ecosystem enhancement is not the core pathway.  
3. Edge cases applied: sustainable procurement can include co-benefits such as waste reduction, local economic development, or resilience-oriented sourcing, but under this canonical mechanism the defining effect is emissions reduction in the supply chain, so Mitigation remains primary.  
4. Confirming applicability across all instances: any policy sharing this mechanism—regardless of sector, purchaser type, or geography—works through lowering the embodied emissions of procured goods/services, so the same primary category applies across instances.  
5. Secondary subtype assignment is deferred to policy-level evidence: specific subtypes such as circular procurement, low-carbon concrete, food procurement, or supplier disclosure programs depend on the exact policy design and should be assigned later based on policy-specific evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Edge case: some sustainable procurement policies are framed around cost savings, waste minimization, circular economy, biodiversity, or resilience of supply chains. Those variants could imply Resource Efficiency, Nature-Based Solutions, or Adaptation co-benefits, but when the canonical mechanism is explicitly supply-chain emissions reduction, Mitigation is the correct primary classification.
key_indicators: "By 2030"; "reduce greenhouse gas emissions"; "institutional, commercial, and government purchasing"; "by at least 50%"
co_benefits: Possible ancillary benefits include reduced air pollution, resource efficiency, and potentially lower water/material footprints from cleaner procurement choices, but none are explicitly stated.
instance_edge_case_notes: This could be implemented through procurement requirements or performance standards if additional text specified mandatory purchasing rules, supplier criteria, or embodied-carbon limits. In the provided wording alone, it reads as a quantified procurement-emissions target rather than a specific mandate.
